content.text: Approved Runtime and Framework Version Matrix

A GenAI application in production runs across four version-sensitive layers: hardware/driver, compute libraries (CUDA, cuDNN), language runtimes (Python, Node.js), and ML frameworks (PyTorch, LangChain). Without a maintained approved version matrix, individual teams pin whatever version works for their current feature, creating an environment where production runs on a mix of versions that have never been tested together and where security patches in one layer are not applied because doing so would require compatibility work with another layer.

The approved version matrix defines the supported combinations: which versions are approved for production, which are deprecated with an upgrade deadline, and which are not approved and blocked from production deployments.

attrs.alt: GenAI runtime version matrix across hardware, compute, runtime, and framework layers — showing approved versions, evaluation versions, and blocked versions

content.text: Why GenAI Stacks Need Explicit Version Matrices

Standard web applications depend on a few runtime versions (Python, Node.js, a database). GenAI inference stacks add GPU driver, CUDA, cuDNN, and ML framework version as additional dimensions, each with complex compatibility requirements. The combinatorial version space is large enough that 'use the latest version of everything' is not a valid policy.
Three failure modes that explicit version matrices prevent:
: PyTorch 2.4 requires CUDA 12.1+. An environment running CUDA 11.8 cannot use PyTorch 2.4, but this incompatibility often surfaces as a silent inference error rather than a clear error message.
: Python 3.9 reached end-of-life in October 2025 and will not receive further security patches. Without a version policy, services can drift onto EOL runtimes without triggering any alert.

: LangChain 0.1.x to 0.2.x included breaking changes to the core chain interface. Teams that had not pinned to a compatible version range found their applications broken after a routine dependency update.
Structure of the Approved Version Matrix
The matrix has four status categories for each version entry:
None — this is the target state
Being assessed for production approval; not yet approved
Use in non-production environments only; timeline for approval decision defined
Still supported in production but scheduled for removal
Upgrade plan required by the stated deadline
Blocked from production; either EOL, insecure, or not validated
CI gate must prevent deployment if this version is detected

GPU hardware and driver versions are infrastructure-level decisions made by the platform team, not application teams. The matrix documents the approved driver version ranges for each GPU family in production.
Same driver range as A100; check NVLink compatibility separately
Managed by AWS; version controlled via AMI version
Approved for embedding models only; LLM inference not approved on CPU
ECC memory not guaranteed; unsuitable for production inference

Driver versions outside the approved range are not blocked by a CI gate (driver version is read from the host, not from the application build), but are flagged by the weekly infrastructure compliance scan.
Layer 2: CUDA and Compute Library Versions
CUDA, cuDNN, and TensorRT versions must be compatible with each other and with the ML framework. The matrix specifies validated combinations, not individual versions.
Only use validated combination stacks from this table.

Mixing versions across stacks (e.g., CUDA 12.1 with cuDNN 9.0) may work in development but can produce silent numerical precision errors in production inference that are undetectable without output comparison.
Language runtimes have well-documented EOL dates. The approved matrix tracks both the current approved version and the EOL date to enable proactive planning.
3.13 not yet approved; eval in progress
Use LTS versions only; odd versions not approved
For sidecar services only; not primary inference runtime

Python version management is complicated by ML framework compatibility. PyTorch 2.4 supports Python 3.8–3.12; some older model serving frameworks required Python 3.8 long after it was deprecated. The approved matrix serves as the authoritative tie-breaker: if a framework requires a Python version that is not approved, the framework must be updated or replaced, not the Python EOL policy.
Layer 4: ML Framework and LLM SDK Versions

ML framework versions are the most volatile layer of the matrix. PyTorch releases every few months; LLM SDK packages release weekly. The matrix defines the approved minor version range rather than a single pinned version.
A documented matrix with no enforcement is advisory at best. Production deployments must be blocked automatically when they contain non-approved versions.
The version gate in CI checks the application's declared dependencies. Container image scanning adds a second layer that checks the runtime environment baked into the Docker image.

# Dockerfile — pin to an approved base image that locks CUDA and Python together
# The approved base image list is maintained separately from the version matrix
# and is refreshed when new CUDA versions are approved

# Approved: CUDA 12.3 + Python 3.11 (stack-B)
FROM pytorch/pytorch:2.3.1-cuda12.1-cudnn8-runtime

# Verify the matrix combination at build time
RUN python -c "
import torch, sys
assert sys.version_info[:2] == (3, 11), f'Python version {sys.version_info[:2]} not approved'
assert torch.__version__.startswith('2.3'), f'PyTorch {torch.__version__} not approved'
cuda = torch.version.cuda
assert cuda and cuda.startswith('12.'), f'CUDA {cuda} not approved'
print(f'Version gate passed: Python 3.11, PyTorch {torch.__version__}, CUDA {cuda}')
"

WORKDIR /app
COPY requirements.txt .
RUN pip install --no-cache-dir -r requirements.txt

# Run version policy check as part of build — fails build if violated
RUN python scripts/check_version_policy.py

COPY . .

The version matrix is not static. New versions are approved as they are validated; old versions are deprecated when newer ones are available; deprecations become not-approved when EOL dates pass. Managing these transitions requires a process that gives engineering teams advance notice.
A new version moves from Evaluation to Approved through a validation pipeline:
— the new version is deployed to a dedicated evaluation environment. The full golden prompt set, safety regression suite, and performance benchmarks are run against it.
— verify that the new version is compatible with all other approved versions it depends on or is depended upon by (CUDA ↔ PyTorch ↔ ONNX Runtime compatibility matrix).
— run CVE scan on the new version. Confirm no known unpatched vulnerabilities at time of approval.
— the platform team records the approval decision with date, validator name, and validation artifacts (CI run links, benchmark results).
— the matrix document is updated and a notification is sent to engineering teams with the effective date.
When a version is deprecated, engineering teams need time to plan and execute the upgrade. Standard deprecation timelines:
At upstream EOL date or 6 months after deprecation, whichever is later
3 months after successor version is approved
1 month — SDKs update frequently and old versions accumulate security debt
At NVIDIA driver support drop or 6 months, whichever is later

The hard removal deadline is when the not-approved status takes effect and the CI gate begins blocking deployments using the deprecated version. Missing the deadline means the service cannot be deployed until it upgrades — creating an operational emergency that could have been a planned sprint task.

Occasionally a team genuinely cannot upgrade by the deadline — a critical third-party dependency is only compatible with the deprecated version, or the upgrade requires a multi-month migration. The waiver process documents the exception and sets conditions for its resolution.
The specific version being retained and the specific service using it
The technical blocker preventing the upgrade
— additional security measures applied because the normal policy cannot be met (e.g., additional network isolation, manual security review of all changes to the affected service)
— no waiver is indefinite; maximum 90 days with a required renewal if the blocker is not resolved
A waiver is not a path to avoiding the upgrade — it is an acknowledgment that the upgrade is delayed with a plan to complete it.
Services with active waivers are prioritized in the next sprint planning cycle.

Between CI runs, production environments can drift from the approved matrix through infrastructure changes, hotfixes, or dependency auto-updates. Scheduled drift detection scans identify these deviations before they accumulate.
A lightweight agent runs on each inference node every 6 hours and reports installed package versions to a central inventory:
The inventory service evaluates each collected snapshot against the approved version matrix and flags violations:
Violations at CRITICAL severity trigger an immediate alert to the on-call engineer. WARNING violations are aggregated into the weekly upgrade report.

Adding a new version to the matrix requires verifying compatibility not just with the framework that directly uses it, but across the full stack. A CUDA upgrade that breaks ONNX Runtime's TensorRT execution provider is an incompatibility that only surfaces under the full stack combination.
The validation pipeline runs a matrix of stack combinations to surface these cross-layer incompatibilities before an approval decision:

# .github/workflows/stack-compatibility.yml
# Triggered when a version is submitted for Evaluation status
name: Stack Compatibility Validation

on:
  workflow_dispatch:
    inputs:
      candidate_version:
        description: "Package and version to validate (e.g., torch==2.5.0)"
        required: true
      stack_combinations:
        description: "JSON array of stacks to test against"
        required: true

jobs:
  compatibility-matrix:
    runs-on: [self-hosted, gpu, ${{ matrix.runner_tag }}]
    strategy:
      fail-fast: false
      matrix:
        stack: ${{ fromJSON(github.event.inputs.stack_combinations) }}
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4

      - name: Install approved base stack
        run: |
          pip install torch==${{ matrix.stack.torch_version }} \
                      onnxruntime-gpu==${{ matrix.stack.ort_version }} \
                      ${{ github.event.inputs.candidate_version }}

      - name: Run version compatibility smoke tests
        run: python tests/compat/smoke_test.py

      - name: Run golden prompt regression suite
        run: python tests/compat/golden_prompts.py --stack ${{ matrix.stack.id }}

      - name: Run inference performance benchmarks
        run: python tests/compat/benchmark.py --baseline baselines/${{ matrix.stack.id }}.json

      - name: Upload results
        uses: actions/upload-artifact@v4
        with:
          name: compat-results-${{ matrix.stack.id }}
          path: test-results/
The smoke test covers five categories that cross-layer incompatibilities most commonly affect:
— confirms the inference path works end-to-end with the new version combination
— CUDA and TensorRT version changes frequently break the export path before affecting inference
— GPU OOM errors often stem from changed memory management behavior between CUDA versions
— tensor parallelism and data parallelism paths require explicit testing; NCCL version compatibility is a separate concern
— output logits from the reference model on the golden prompt set must be within a defined tolerance threshold of the baseline
The following runbook documents the end-to-end process for adding a new package version to the approved matrix. It defines who does what, in what order, and with what evidence.
— creates a ticket with: package name, target version, reason for upgrade (security patch / feature requirement / EOL of current version), and proposed timeline.
Platform team sets up evaluation environment
— creates an evaluation environment using the candidate version. This environment runs identical inference workloads to production and is accessible to the submitting team.
— the stack-compatibility workflow runs against all approved stack combinations. Results are attached to the ticket. Pipeline must pass with zero failures.
— the candidate version is scanned for CVEs. Any CRITICAL or HIGH CVEs block approval; the package maintainer must release a fix before re-evaluation.
— benchmark results from the evaluation environment must show throughput within 5% of the production baseline and latency p95 within 10%. Regressions beyond these thresholds require justification.
— platform engineering lead records the approval: ticket ID, validation CI run links, benchmark comparison, security scan results, effective date.
— the matrix document is updated in the repository with the new status. The prior approved version moves to Deprecated with its deadline.
— teams are notified of the new approved version, the deprecation of the prior version, and the deadline for upgrading to the new version.
If an approved version causes production incidents after it is deployed, it can be rolled back to Deprecated status with a 48-hour emergency timeline for services to revert:

# Emergency version rollback — run by platform team on-call
# This demotes an approved version back to Deprecated and re-approves the prior version

PACKAGE="torch"
ROLLBACK_TO="2.3.1"     # prior approved version
DEMOTE_FROM="2.4.0"     # version causing incidents

# 1. Update the matrix document
git checkout -b "emergency-rollback-${PACKAGE}-${DEMOTE_FROM}"
# Edit version-matrix.yaml to set torch 2.4.x back to Deprecated
# and set torch 2.3.x back to Approved

# 2. Notify engineering teams immediately via incident channel
curl -X POST "${SLACK_WEBHOOK}" -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{
    "text": ":rotating_light: EMERGENCY: torch 2.4.0 rolled back to Deprecated. Revert to torch==2.3.1 within 48 hours. See incident #INC-0042."
  }'

# 3. CI gate already blocks new deployments with the demoted version
# Services still running 2.4.0 are tracked by the inventory scan

# 4. Merge matrix update
git add version-matrix.yaml
git commit -m "emergency(matrix): rollback torch 2.4.0 to deprecated — incident #INC-0042"
git push -u origin "emergency-rollback-${PACKAGE}-${DEMOTE_FROM}"
# Open PR for immediate review and merge

The approved runtime and framework version matrix defines which software versions are permitted in production and which are blocked. For GenAI stacks, this spans four layers — GPU drivers, compute libraries (CUDA/cuDNN), language runtimes, and ML frameworks — each with its own compatibility requirements and deprecation timeline.

Enforcement through a CI version gate converts the matrix from a document into a control: unapproved versions block deployment automatically, eliminating the failure mode where a non-approved version reaches production undetected. The deprecation timeline policy — with notice periods of one to six months depending on the layer — gives engineering teams the runway to upgrade proactively rather than reactively.

The matrix is a living document. Version promotion (Evaluation → Approved) and version retirement (Deprecated → Not Approved) happen on a continuous cycle driven by upstream releases, security disclosures, and compatibility validation. Keeping it current is a platform engineering function that pays dividends in reduced incident frequency and faster emergency patch execution.
